what_it_does: Headless Node.js test that stubs MediaRecorder, STT, audio playback, and an ndjson stream reader to exercise the voice session control-flow: per-turn fresh-recorder re-arm, accumulation across pauses without stopping, barge-in cancellation of the reader/audio sources, and an epoch guard that drops chunks decoded after a barge-in.
observations: No real microphone, audio, React, or tldraw is used; everything is stubbed for deterministic control-flow verification.
StubRecorder uses a serial id and an everHadHeader flag to model the webm container header behavior that caused the old turn-2 decode failure.
sttDecode returns an empty string for headerless blobs, reproducing the reported symptom in a controlled way.
listenOnce creates a fresh recorder every call and snapshots while running, only calling stop() at the fire boundary.
sessionLoop repeatedly listens, speaks, and re-arms, with a watcher that can inject a barge-in during a specific turn.
bargeIn increments a play epoch, stops live sources, cancels the current reader, and logs the event.
playWavBuffer defers scheduling with a microtask and drops the chunk if the epoch changed underneath it.
The file ends with explicit PASS/FAIL checks and process.exit(1) on failure.
standouts: The 'teeth' regression block that manually reproduces the old reused-recorder headerless-turn-2 symptom and asserts the new flow avoids it.
The epoch-based async decode guard that proves a chunk finishing after a barge-in is dropped rather than played.
The multi-segment accumulation test showing a not-finished pause keeps the recorder running so the whole thought is captured.
conventions: Simple console.log PASS/FAIL harness instead of an external test framework.
Stubs are named to mirror the real primitives they stand in for (e.g., runVoiceTurn, playWavBuffer, listenOnce).
Comments explicitly scope what is proven and what is not.
concerns: The watcher helper is minimal and only injects barge-in after a single microtask; it does not model arbitrary mid-stream timing.
sessionLoop increments rearms unconditionally after runVoiceTurn, so the metric does not distinguish barge-in completion from normal completion.
The old-bug reproduction mutates the StubRecorder internals directly, so it tests the stub model as much as the real code path.
notes: A deterministic, stub-driven control-flow proof for voice auto-listen re-arm and barge-in behavior, with explicit regression checks against the old reused-recorder bug.
questions: Which production file(s) implement the real counterparts of listenOnce, runVoiceTurn, playWavBuffer, and bargeIn?
How does the actual bridge translate reader.cancel() into MSG_PEEK and stop synthesis?
What is the real VAD/judge interface, and does it match the alwaysFinished/segJudge stubs used here?
How is a barge-in signal detected in production, and is the epoch guard present in the real playWavBuffer implementation?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches: the file is exactly a headless control-flow proof for re-arm and barge-in, and its comments correctly scope what is not proven (real mic, VAD, audible cutoff, iOS audio).
apparent_themes: Deterministic control-flow verification via lightweight stubs
Regression testing with explicit reproduction of the old failure mode
Fresh resource allocation per cycle to avoid stale state
Epoch-based cancellation for async audio decode races
intention_signals: To provide a maintainable, fast, deterministic regression harness that captures the essential invariants of the voice session loop without requiring real audio hardware or UI, while documenting the boundary between what is proven and what remains device/integration tested.
novelty: It models the webm header state of a MediaRecorder with a serial-numbered stub and uses that model to prove that a fresh recorder per turn prevents the headerless turn-2 decode regression, plus an epoch guard for async audio decode cancellation.
contribution: A set of deterministic control-flow assertions and stub contracts that a fused system would keep to guard against regressions in per-turn recorder re-arming, speech accumulation across pauses, and barge-in cancellation of playback and decoding.
summary_for_embedding: Headless Node.js test stubbing MediaRecorder, STT, and audio playback to prove voice session-loop re-arm and barge-in control-flow invariants with regression checks.
intention_for_embedding: To provide a deterministic, file-local control-flow proof that the voice auto-listen loop creates a fresh recorder each turn, preserves accumulation across pauses, and correctly cancels playback on barge-in.

symbols.does: Logs PASS/FAIL for a named assertion and increments the failure counter.
symbols.description: The minimal assertion helper used throughout the proof blocks; it prints a PASS or FAIL line and bumps a global failures counter that drives the final exit code.

symbols.does: Models a MediaRecorder that emits a container header on start and can be snapshotted while running.
symbols.description: A stub recorder with a serial id, segment buffer, and an everHadHeader flag. start() marks the header as present, push() adds speech segments, snapshot() reports whether the blob is headerless and the joined text, and stop() flushes the result through an optional callback.

symbols.does: Decodes a stub blob to text, returning empty for headerless blobs.
symbols.description: Mirrors the real STT behavior where a continuation blob with no container header decodes to empty, which is the controlled reproduction of the reported turn-2 symptom.

symbols.does: Bumps the play epoch and stops all tracked audio sources.
symbols.description: Called during barge-in to invalidate any in-flight async decode and to tear down live playback sources so the next turn can start fresh.

symbols.does: Sets the barged flag, stops playback, cancels the current reader, and logs the event.
symbols.description: The stub equivalent of a user interrupt during synthesis; it coordinates epoch invalidation, source teardown, and reader cancellation in one operation.

symbols.does: Schedules an audio source after a microtask unless the epoch changed mid-decode.
symbols.description: Models the real async audio decode/play path. It captures the current epoch, yields once, and only schedules a source if no barge-in has bumped the epoch in the meantime.

symbols.does: Consumes a stub ndjson reader and plays chunks until done or barge-in.
symbols.description: Mirrors the reply playback loop: it creates a cancellable reader, reads chunks, plays chunk messages, and exits early if ref.barged becomes true or the reader is cancelled.

symbols.does: Runs one listen cycle with a fresh recorder, accumulating segments until the judge says finished.
symbols.description: The core re-arm fix in stub form: each call creates a new StubRecorder, pushes speech segments, snapshots while running (so the header is present), decodes, and only stops at the fire boundary. A null result means the loop went deaf, reproducing the old symptom.

symbols.does: Orchestrates listen, speak, and re-arm across a sequence of turns.
symbols.description: The fixed session loop under test. It runs listenOnce for each turn, plays the reply via runVoiceTurn with an optional barge-in watcher, and counts fires and rearms so the proof can assert continuous listening across multiple turns.

symbols.does: Async helper that optionally triggers bargeIn on a configured turn.
symbols.description: Created inside sessionLoop for each speaking phase; if bargeInOnTurn matches the current turn it yields once and then calls bargeIn(), otherwise it resolves quickly.

symbols.does: Judge stub that always returns finished.
symbols.description: Used in the multi-turn re-arm and barge-in proofs so that the first speech segment immediately fires a turn.

symbols.does: Judge stub that returns finished once the text contains 'settings'.
symbols.description: Used in the accumulation proof to force a not-finished verdict on the first segment and a finished verdict on the second, verifying that the recorder kept running and both segments are preserved.
